title: User Guide
text:
Dittonomics keeps the original research workflow logic:

the main Codex session acts as orchestrator
narrow subagents handle bounded specialist roles
creators create, critics review
the workflow advances only when artifacts clear their quality gates

What changes is the implementation surface. Instead of relying on Claude-specific configuration layers, the Codex adaptation uses:

AGENTS.md and repo overlays
Codex skills with agents/openai.yaml
Codex custom subagents in TOML
explicit workflow references

title: User Guide
text:
Phase 1: Discovery
Discovery should produce:

a research specification
a literature map
a data assessment

The relevant roles are:

librarian and librarian_critic
explorer and explorer_critic

The usual explicit entry point is $clo-discover.

title: User Guide
text:
Phase 2: Strategy
Strategy locks the estimand, design, assumptions, falsification logic, and robustness plan before coding starts.
The relevant roles are:

strategist
strategist_critic

The usual explicit entry point is $clo-strategize.

title: User Guide
text:
Phase 3: Execution
Execution translates the design into:

cleaned data products
analysis scripts
tables and figures
paper sections

The relevant roles are:

data_engineer
coder
coder_critic
writer
writer_critic

The active default language stance in this adaptation is:

Python primary
Stata co-default
R and Julia supported when the repo or the user explicitly centers them

title: User Guide
text:
Phase 4: Peer Review
Peer review is not a grammar pass. It is where the workflow asks whether the project is credible, scoped correctly, journal-calibrated, and ready to move forward.
The relevant roles are:

editor
domain_referee
methods_referee

The usual explicit entry point is $clo-review.

title: User Guide
text:
Phase 5: Submission
Submission requires more than polished prose. The workflow should verify:

replication readiness
journal fit
final package completeness

The relevant role is:

verifier

The usual explicit entry point is $clo-submit.

title: User Guide
text:
Minimum rule
If exploratory work is needed and explorations/ does not exist, create:

explorations/README.md
explorations/ARCHIVE/

Then create a subfolder per exploration.

title: User Guide
text:
Graduation rule
Once an exploration becomes part of the real pipeline, promote the relevant outputs into the repo’s canonical code, output, docs, or manuscript paths and leave a short record of what graduated.

title: User Guide
text:
The upstream guide says CLAUDE.md
Translate that to:

AGENTS.md
AGENTS.override.md
repo-local skills

title: User Guide
text:
The upstream guide mentions .claude/rules
Translate that to:

active workflow references
source-rule mirrors when provenance matters
repo overlays for truly always-on repo behavior

title: User Guide
text:
The workflow feels too implicit
Use explicit skill mentions such as $clo-review or $clo-discover instead of relying on automatic inference.

title: User Guide
section: The repo lacks an exploration sandbox
text:
The repo lacks an exploration sandbox
Create explorations/README.md and explorations/ARCHIVE/ before running prototype work.

title: Dittonomics
text:
Dittonomics is a Codex-native research workflow that openly borrows from multiple upstream repos instead of pretending it was invented from scratch.
The name is deliberate: like Ditto, this project copies useful traits, reshapes them, and turns them into one portable operating model for empirical work in Codex.
The goal is simple: preserve the workflow logic that makes the upstream systems useful for research, while translating the active instructions into Codex-native primitives:

title: Dittonomics
text:
Built From
Dittonomics builds its architecture, workflow method, and prompting style from:

Clo-Author by Hugo Sant’Anna
my_claude_skills by Daria M.
claudeblattman by Chris Blattman

This is a remix on purpose. The project is called Dittonomics because it copies patterns from different repos and adapts them into one Codex-facing research system.

title: Dittonomics
text:
What You Get

a five-phase research pipeline
worker-critic separation across the whole workflow
journal-aware peer review via editor plus blind referees
a Codex migration layer that makes the upstream material readable without constant translation
an example overlay that shows how one concrete installation and research setup can adapt the generic workflow
a public .codex/ starter kit that can be copied or adapted after forking

title: Dittonomics
text:
Get Started in 5 Minutes

Step 1: Understand the starter model
This repo ships a forkable starter layer under .codex/ with:

AGENTS.md
agents/
skills/
WORKFLOW_QUICK_REF.md

When you adopt it for your own machine, the main user-level Codex storage layers are:

user skills in ~/.codex/skills
custom subagents in ~/.codex/agents

Repo-specific overrides still belong in:

AGENTS.md
AGENTS.override.md
.agents/skills/



Step 2: Translate the upstream mental model
If the upstream manuals say:

CLAUDE.md -> read or create AGENTS.md
.claude/rules -> use active workflow references plus repo overlay guidance
/discover -> use $clo-discover
.claude/agents -> use Codex custom subagents

The public migration cheatsheet lives in Reference.


Step 3: Create or adapt a repo
For a new research repo, the minimum Codex-facing structure is:

root AGENTS.md
optional AGENTS.override.md
repo-local skills in .agents/skills/
canonical manuscript, code, output, and docs directories
explorations/ when the project needs a sandbox for prototypes



Step 4: Use the workflow phases
The workflow stays the same:

Discovery
Strategy
Execution
Peer Review
Submission

Talks can run in parallel with review.


Step 5: Start with explicit skills
The safest Codex-native entry points are:

$clo-discover
$clo-strategize
$clo-analyze
$clo-write
$clo-review
$clo-submit

The architecture page explains how these map to agents and quality gates.

title: Dittonomics
text:
Prerequisites








Tool
Why it matters
Notes




Codex
Main orchestrator
Desktop or terminal usage both fit the architecture


Python
Default automation and analysis language
Primary default in this adaptation


Stata
Econometric co-default
Use when the repo or replication target centers Stata


XeLaTeX
Paper compilation
Needed for LaTeX manuscripts and slides


Git
Versioning and review workflow
Required for repo-based operation


Quarto
Docs site only
Needed to render this manual locally



R and Julia remain supported, but they are no longer the default recommendation in the active Codex adaptation.

title: Dittonomics
text:
What This Site Is Not
This site is not:

a literal clone of any one upstream repo
a promise that Codex supports hidden .claude mechanics
a packaged installer for one exact workstation

It is a public operating manual for a Codex-native remix of several upstream research-workflow systems.

title: Dittonomics
text:
Attribution
Dittonomics is adapted from the Claude-based Clo-Author by Hugo Sant’Anna, plus ideas and patterns drawn from my_claude_skills and claudeblattman.
The aim here is adaptation, not concealment. The project works by being explicit about what it borrows and how it rewrites those pieces for Codex.

title: Architecture
text:
The upstream architecture Dittonomics builds from is powerful because it separates:

project constitution
always-needed workflow rules
on-demand references
on-demand workflows
specialist agents
infrastructure behavior

The Codex adaptation keeps that shape, but translates the layers into Codex-native surfaces.








Layer
Codex-native location
Loaded when




Session constitution
AGENTS.md
every session in the repo


Repo overlay
AGENTS.override.md
every session when present


Repo-local skills
.agents/skills/
on demand or by explicit mention


Forkable starter bundle
.codex/ in this repo
when a user copies or adapts the public starter


User skills
~/.codex/skills
on demand or implicit by policy


Custom subagents
~/.codex/agents
when the main session delegates


Explicit utilities and config
scripts, references, config.toml
when invoked



The important design difference is that Codex should not be documented as if hidden Claude hooks still exist. If behavior matters, it needs an explicit home.
The public repo therefore carries two distinct layers:

a forkable .codex/ starter kit for users who want an upstream-style distribution folder
repo-native AGENTS.md, AGENTS.override.md, and .agents/skills/ patterns for actual project customization

title: Architecture
text:
Agents by Phase
The structure remains worker -> critic:

Discovery: librarian -> librarian_critic, explorer -> explorer_critic
Strategy: strategist -> strategist_critic
Execution: data_engineer or coder or writer -> paired critics
Peer Review: editor plus two blind referees
Submission: verifier as final gate

title: Architecture
text:
Talks
Talk creation remains parallel to peer review, not a final afterthought.

title: Architecture
text:
Replication-first coding
The Codex adaptation should still encourage:

stable file naming
reproducible outputs
explicit serialization of reusable intermediates
review before promotion into manuscript assets

title: Architecture
text:
Exploration-first side work
explorations/ is part of the architecture, not a convenience folder. It gives prototype work an explicit home outside the canonical pipeline.

title: Agents
text:
The Codex adaptation keeps the Clo-Author specialist structure, but the implementation format changes:

upstream markdown agent definitions become Codex custom subagents
the main Codex session remains the orchestrator
skills decide when to dispatch roles

Each role should stay narrow. The architecture works because workers do the making and critics do the checking.

title: Agents
text:
Librarian
Creates:

literature maps
annotated bibliographies
frontier summaries

title: Agents
text:
Librarian Critic
Checks:

coverage gaps
recency
scope discipline
journal relevance

title: Agents
text:
Explorer
Creates:

data source lists
feasibility rankings
variable-coverage assessments

title: Agents
text:
Explorer Critic
Checks:

measurement validity
sample selection
external validity
design compatibility

title: Agents
text:
Strategist
Designs:

estimand
identification strategy
robustness plan
falsification tests

title: Agents
text:
Strategist Critic
Checks:

design validity
assumptions
inference logic
failure modes

title: Agents
text:
Data Engineer
Handles:

cleaning
wrangling
figure data preparation
reproducible output structure

title: Agents
text:
Coder
Handles:

estimation scripts
table generation
analysis orchestration

title: Agents
text:
Coder Critic
Checks:

strategy alignment
reproducibility
code quality
output readiness

title: Agents
text:
Writer
Handles:

paper sections
notation discipline
claims-and-evidence alignment

title: Agents
text:
Writer Critic
Checks:

hedging
notation drift
claim inflation
style consistency

title: Agents
text:
Editor
The editor does not simply summarize. The editor:

calibrates scope
decides whether the paper is appropriately targeted
assigns the referee posture
synthesizes the decision after reports come back

title: Agents
text:
Domain Referee
Checks:

contribution
literature positioning
external validity
field expectations

title: Agents
text:
Methods Referee
Checks:

identification
estimation
inference
robustness sufficiency

title: Agents
text:
Storyteller and Storyteller Critic
These roles run in the presentation workflow and focus on:

deck structure
narrative arc
figure fidelity
talk format quality

title: Agents
text:
Verifier
The verifier is the final mechanical gatekeeper:

runability
package completeness
replication readiness
output freshness

title: Customization
text:
Dittonomics is designed to be customized without losing the architecture.
The main rule is:

keep generic user-level defaults generic
keep project-specific behavior in repo overlays
keep provenance available but separate from active operating guidance

title: Customization
text:
Install layout

starter bundle in the public repo: .codex/
user skills after installation: ~/.codex/skills
custom subagents after installation: ~/.codex/agents
user-level constitution after installation: ~/.codex/AGENTS.md

title: Customization
text:
Active workflow defaults

Python primary
Stata co-default
R and Julia supported when explicitly needed
journal calibration is open-ended and can be adapted to any target journal set
one example overlay may include Journal of Development Economics, Regional Studies, and Journal of Economic Geography to show how a user can customize the reference layer

title: Customization
text:
Repo overlay pattern
For a project repo:

put stable repo rules in AGENTS.md
put project-specific deviations in AGENTS.override.md
put repo-only skills in .agents/skills/
create explorations/ when the project needs a prototype sandbox

title: Customization
text:
Example research orientation
This example overlay is designed around empirical economics and adjacent social science workflows, not general software engineering alone.
